coredata.dc:title: Highly efficient dye adsorption and sunlight-driven photocatalytic degradation using electrospun PAN/CDs@BN fibrous membranes

coredata.dc:description: Boron nitride (BN) is a promising material for wastewater treatment because of its unique characteristics. However, its wide bandgap presents a major limitation, hindering its photocatalytic activity when exposed to solar irradiation. Herein, we present a sustainable strategy for modifying BN using biomass-derived carbon dots (CDs). The resulting CDs@BN composite was integrated into electrospun polyacrylonitrile (PAN) substrates to enhance dye adsorption and photocatalytic degradation under sunlight exposure. The CDs were synthesized and coated on BN using a hydrothermal method. The CDs@BN composite was then incorporated into an electrospinning solution to fabricate electrospun PAN/CDs@BN fibers. The optimized samples exhibited significantly improved adsorption capacities of 220.79 and 198.81 mg/g for methylene blue (MB) and rhodamine B (Rh B), respectively, which were nearly double those of uncoated BN. Additionally, the enhanced photocatalytic degradation performance resulted in removal efficiencies of 99.50 % for MB and 85.90 % for Rh B after one hour of solar irradiation. These optimized samples also demonstrated excellent stability and reusability. Mechanistic investigations revealed a synergistic interaction between the CDs and BN, with the CDs effectively mitigating the intrinsic limitations of BN, thereby contributing to performance enhancement. This work provides valuable insights into the development of advanced BN-based functional materials for wastewater treatment applications.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the product manufacturing processes for dye adsorption and sunlight-driven photocatalytic degradation: (a) fabrication of CDs@BN and (b) fabrication of PAN/CDs@BN, featuring fibers with a unique protrusion-like structure and perforated morphology and demonstrating excellent dye removal performance.

Physicochemical characterization of the sample. (a) TEM image of the CDs (the inset shows the size distribution). (b) HR-TEM image of CDs (the inset shows a magnified view). (c) SEM image of CDs@BN. (d) Element mapping of PAN/CDs@BN15. (e) SEM image of PAN/CDs@BN15 (the inset shows a magnified view, where purple represents CDs@BN and orange represents the pore structure). (f) Diameter analysis of the samples. (g) FTIR spectra and (h) XRD pattern of representative samples.
Evaluation of the adsorption performance of the samples for MB and Rh B. (a, b) Effect of contact time. (c, d) Effect of initial dye concentration.

Effect of different adsorption environments on the adsorption capacity of the PAN/CDs@BN15 sample. (a) Effect of pH value, (b) temperature, and (c) recycling. (d) Performance comparison between the results of this work and previously reported immobilization of different functional materials using electrospun substrates for MB and Rh B adsorption.

Mechanism of the excellent adsorption performance of the samples. (a) FTIR spectra of the PAN/CDs@BN15 sample before and after dye adsorption. (b, c) C1s and O1s XPS spectra of the PAN/CDs@BN15 sample before and after dye adsorption. (d) Nitrogen adsorption–desorption isotherms, (e) pore characteristics, (f) BET surface area, and Barrett–Joyner–Halenda (BJH) pore volume of representative samples. (g) Wetting behavior of representative samples. (h) Schematic of the dye adsorption mechanism of the samples.

Evaluation of light-assisted photocatalytic dye degradation performance of the samples. (a) MB removal performance of the samples in different environments. (b) Photocatalytic degradation profile of MB and (c) time-dependent UV–Vis spectra of MB using the PAN/CDs@BN15 sample as a catalyst under sunlight exposure. (d) Rh B removal performance of samples in different environments. (e) Photocatalytic degradation profile of Rh B and (f) time-dependent UV–Vis spectra of Rh B using the PAN/CDs@BN15 sample as a catalyst under sunlight exposure. (g) Analysis of the degradation rate of dyes using the PAN/CDs@BN15 sample. (h) Effects of recycling on degradation performance. (i) Performance comparison between previously reported immobilization of different functional materials using electrospun substrates for MB and Rh B photocatalytic degradation and the results of the current work.

Investigation of the superior photocatalytic degradation performance mechanisms of the samples. (a) UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra of BN and CDs@BN (the inset shows Tauc's plot). (b) Photocurrent plot. (c) EIS plot. (d) Effect of different scavengers on the photocatalytic activity of PAN/CDs@BN15. (e) Analysis of color differences between the samples. (f) UV–vis reflectance spectra of the samples. (g) Infrared photographs of the samples after applying light and the temperature variation curve with time. (h) Schematic of the dye photocatalytic degradation mechanism of the samples.

head.ce:title: Highly efficient dye adsorption and sunlight-driven photocatalytic degradation using electrospun PAN/CDs@BN fibrous membranes

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Boron nitride (BN) is a promising material for wastewater treatment because of its unique characteristics. However, its wide bandgap presents a major limitation, hindering its photocatalytic activity when exposed to solar irradiation. Herein, we present a sustainable strategy for modifying BN using biomass-derived carbon dots (CDs). The resulting CDs@BN composite was integrated into electrospun polyacrylonitrile (PAN) substrates to enhance dye adsorption and photocatalytic degradation under sunlight exposure. The CDs were synthesized and coated on BN using a hydrothermal method. The CDs@BN composite was then incorporated into an electrospinning solution to fabricate electrospun PAN/CDs@BN fibers. The optimized samples exhibited significantly improved adsorption capacities of 220.79 and 198.81 mg/g for methylene blue (MB) and rhodamine B (Rh B), respectively, which were nearly double those of uncoated BN. Additionally, the enhanced photocatalytic degradation performance resulted in removal efficiencies of 99.50 % for MB and 85.90 % for Rh B after one hour of solar irradiation. These optimized samples also demonstrated excellent stability and reusability. Mechanistic investigations revealed a synergistic interaction between the CDs and BN, with the CDs effectively mitigating the intrinsic limitations of BN, thereby contributing to performance enhancement. This work provides valuable insights into the development of advanced BN-based functional materials for wastewater treatment applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Dyes are widely utilized in the textile, tannery, and paper industries. However, the toxic properties of most dyes and their resistance to biodegradation present significant environmental risks when dye effluents are discharged into water sources [1]. Current methods for addressing dye contamination in wastewater include adsorption [2], ion exchange [3], catalysis [4], and separation [5]. Among these methods, adsorption is more recognized because of its high efficiency and cost-effectiveness [6]. However, adsorption only transfers dyes from wastewater to the adsorbent, forming a dye-containing complex that requires additional treatment and regeneration, which could potentially lead to secondary pollution and increased costs [7]. In contrast, photocatalytic degradation is a sustainable technology that harnesses solar energy to create oxidizing species capable of effectively degrading dye molecules into carbon dioxide and water [8]. However, improving the efficiency of catalysts for the photocatalytic degradation of dyes in wastewater remains challenging [9]. The synergy between adsorption and photocatalysis facilitates contact between pollutants and catalysts, thereby enhancing photocatalytic activity. Additionally, the degradation of pollutants on the adsorbent surface ensures complete detoxification, while also enabling the sustainable reuse of the adsorbent [10,11]. Consequently, the development of composite materials that combine both adsorption and photocatalytic capabilities is essential for achieving efficient and complete dye removal in wastewater treatment [12–14].

Hexagonal boron nitride (BN) is a two-dimensional material characterized by a graphite-like structure. BN-based adsorbents have been widely used in wastewater treatment applications owing to the excellent physicochemical properties of BN, which include high specific surface area, large pore volume, superb oxidation resistance and high chemical stability [15]. However, the wide bandgap of BN (approximately 5.0–6.0 eV) poses a challenge, as even ultraviolet (UV) light from natural sunlight is insufficient to excite electrons from the valence band to the conduction band, making it difficult to use BN solely as a photocatalyst [16]. Fortunately, this limitation can be addressed by incorporating co-catalysts to form BN-based nanocomposites [17]. For example, coupling BN with TiO2, a widely used photocatalyst, can reduce the bandgap of the composite, thereby significantly enhancing its photocatalytic activity for the degradation of rhodamine B (Rh B) [18]. Similarly, the BN/α-Fe2O3 nanocomposite has been employed as a photocatalyst for the removal of methylene blue (MB). The composite exhibited excellent photocatalytic degradation performance owing to its excellent visible-light harvesting capacity and efficient electron–hole pair separation [19]. Despite the enhanced photocatalytic efficiency achieved through the incorporation of co-catalysts in BN, it is important to note that many co-catalysts contain metallic elements. The presence of these metal-containing photocatalysts in water treatment has raised concerns due to their potential to dissolve and cause secondary contamination [20]. Hence, the development of metal-free co-catalysts that can enhance the photocatalytic performance of BN and prevent secondary contamination is crucial for advancing sustainable water treatment technologies.

Potential metal-free co-catalysts for enhancing the photocatalytic performance of BN include graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) [21], covalent organic frameworks (COFs) [22], and polymeric semiconductors [23]. Despite their benefits, these materials have inherent limitations that hinder their overall effectiveness. For instance, g-C3N4 has a low charge separation efficiency and exhibits rapid electron–hole recombination [21]. Although COFs offer highly tunable structures, their restricted light absorption capacity and propensity for rapid charge recombination often necessitate extensive modifications to achieve practicability [22]. Similarly, polymeric semiconductors exhibit narrow light absorption ranges and moderate charge transfer efficiency [23]. Carbon dots (CDs) are emerging eco-friendly carbon nanomaterials that have been widely used as photocatalysts for the degradation of water pollutants in recent years [24]. Owing to their unique photophysical and chemical properties, CDs are potential solutions to the limitations of metal-free co-catalysts. Their broad-spectrum light absorption and distinctive upconversion fluorescence enable the efficient utilization of both visible and near-infrared light, making them highly suitable for modifying traditional catalysts with large bandgaps, such as CDs@TiO2 [25] and CDs@ZnO [26]. This underscores the potential of CDs for effectively mitigating the spectral constraints inherent to COFs and polymeric semiconductors. Moreover, the intrinsic ability of CDs to suppress charge recombination through dynamic electron-donor and electron-acceptor properties surpasses the performance of g-C3N4 and other commonly used co-catalysts [27]. Additionally, the surface of CDs is enriched with abundant functional groups, including amino, hydroxyl, and carboxylic groups, which provide numerous active adsorption sites. These features facilitate robust interactions with pollutant molecules, thereby significantly enhancing the adsorption capacity of the material [28]. Furthermore, CDs are easy to synthesize, robust under operational conditions, and do not require extensive post-modifications [24]. Hence, CDs can serve as ideal modifiers for BN, potentially forming a highly efficient CDs@BN composite. This composite is anticipated to exhibit remarkable dye adsorption and photocatalytic degradation performance in wastewater treatment. The synergistic interaction between CDs and BN is expected to significantly enhance adsorption capacity, with the superior photocatalytic properties of CDs addressing the limitations of BN (such as limited visible light absorption). This synergy is expected to enable the composite to degrade dyes across a broader light spectrum, thereby boosting its overall performance.

Although CDs@BN composites demonstrate substantial potential for dye removal, their particulate nature presents several practical challenges. Particle aggregation induced by van der Waals interactions, coupled with the complexity of separating these particles from wastewater post-treatment, reduces their overall efficiency and introduces risks of secondary contamination [29]. To overcome these limitations, the immobilization of functional materials onto substrates has emerged as a promising approach, offering enhanced treatment efficiency, waste reduction, and reduced operating costs [30]. Among the various substrate options, electrospun substrates are particularly advantageous. Their high surface area and tunable porosity facilitate efficient loading and improved exposure of functional materials [31]. Moreover, the structural versatility of electrospun fibers enables the customization of surface properties to enhance specific interactions with target contaminants, thereby optimizing adsorption or catalytic performance [32]. Therefore, the immobilization of CDs@BN on electrospun substrates is anticipated to not only improve recycling efficiency but also boost pollutant removal by enhancing surface interactions and minimizing material loss, thus presenting a sustainable and economical approach.

In this study, we introduce the novel application of CDs onto BN surfaces and the subsequent immobilization of CDs@BN into electrospun polyacrylonitrile (PAN) substrates to achieve highly efficient dye adsorption and photocatalytic degradation. Apple peels were selected as the CD source because of their low cost and sustainability as biomass materials. The PAN/CDs@BN composite was fabricated using one-step electrospinning method by incorporating CDs@BN into the electrospinning solution. The effect of the CDs@BN content in the composite on the adsorption and photocatalytic degradation performance for MB and Rh B was systematically investigated. The optimized composite demonstrated significantly enhanced adsorption and photocatalytic degradation capabilities for both dyes. The underlying mechanism for the superior dye removal performance of the composite was extensively discussed.

PAN (Mw = 150,000 g/mol), hexagonal BN powder, MB solution (15 g/L), hydrochloric acid (HCl; 37 %), and sodium hydroxide (NaOH) powder were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF; 99.9 %) and ethanol (99.9 %) were supplied by Samchun Chemical Co., Ltd. Rh B powder, a 0.01 M−EDTA−2Na standard solution (EDTA), 1,4- benzoquinone (BQ) powder, and isopropanol (IPA; 99.7 %) were obtained from Daejung Chemical Co., Ltd. Fresh apples were acquired from a local supermarket (Gwangju City, Republic of Korea). All the chemicals were used as received without further purification.

Apple peels were used as the carbon source to synthesize CDs through a one-step hydrothermal carbonization process. Fig. 1 a shows a schematic of the synthesis process for the CDs, which followed a previously reported procedure, with minor modifications [33,34]. The peeled apple skins were initially dried in an oven at 60 °C for 24 h, followed by pulverization into a fine powder using a kitchen grinder. Subsequently, 2 g of the dried apple peel powder was dispersed in 50 mL of deionized (DI) water and subjected to ultrasonication for 20 min. The resulting suspension was then transferred into a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated to 200 °C for 12 h. The solution was left to cool naturally before being filtered through a 0.22 µm membrane to yield a solution containing CDs. Finally, the CD-containing filtrate was centrifuged at 12,000 rpm for 15 min to remove residual solids. The resulting CD solution was utilized in the subsequent BN coating process.

To prepare CDs@BN, 50 mg of BN powder was dispersed in 50 mL of the CD solution and sonicated for 20 min to ensure a uniform dispersion of BN. The resulting solution was then transferred to a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 3 h. After natural cooling, the CDs@BN solids were collected via centrifugation at 12,000 rpm for 15 min. The collected CDs@BN solids were subsequently rinsed three times with DI water to remove any residual CD solution. Finally, the CDs@BN solids were dried in an oven at 60 °C for 6 h. A schematic representation of the overall preparation process is shown in Fig. 1a.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the electrospun PAN/CDs@BN fibers
ce:section.ce:para: A specified amount of CDs@BN was initially added to the DMF solution and subsequently ultrasonicated to ensure uniform dispersion of CDs@BN. PAN particles were then introduced into the solution to prepare a PAN/CDs@BN electrospinning solution. The concentration of PAN was maintained at 10 wt%, while the mass ratios of CDs@BN added were controlled at 5, 10, and 15 wt%, relative to the mass of PAN. The prepared solutions were subsequently transferred into syringes for the electrospinning process. During electrospinning, a high voltage of 15 kV was applied to the nozzle, and the distance between the grounded collector and the nozzle was set to 10 cm. Furthermore, the feed rate of the polymer solution was maintained at 0.5 mL/h. All experiments were conducted at 20 °C. The resulting samples were labelled PAN/CDs@BN5, PAN/CDs@BN10, and PAN/CDs@BN15, corresponding to CDs@BN concentrations of 5, 10, and 15 wt%, respectively. Additionally, a sample with 15 wt% pure BN was prepared as a reference, denoted as PAN/BN15.

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM; Gemini 500 + EDS, Oxford) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM; Tecnai G2 F20) were used to confirm the morphological characteristics of the samples. Energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) images of the products were captured using SEM. The fiber diameter information was obtained by randomly selecting at least 100 individual fibers in different SEM images using image visualization software (ImageJ; National Institutes of Health). Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR; Spectrum 400, PerkinElmer) was used to identify the functional groups of the samples. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS; VG Multilab, 2000; Thermo VG Scientific) was used to characterize the surface chemistry of the samples. The crystal structure of the sample was determined using X-ray diffraction (XRD; PANalytical X-ray diffractometer). The pore characteristics and specific surface area of the sample were confirmed using nitrogen adsorption–desorption analysis (Belsorp mini analyzer). UV–visible (UV–Vis) spectra of the samples were analyzed using a spectrophotometer (UV-1900i, Shimadzu). The characteristic peaks of MB and Rh B were observed at 664 and 554 nm, respectively. Fluorescence characteristic analysis was performed on a photoluminescence fluorescence spectrometer (FluoroLog-QM, Horiba). Electrochemical analysis was conducted using an electrochemical workstation (WizEIS-1200Premium) with a standard three-electrode system. Pt and Ag/AgCl electrodes were used as counter and reference electrodes, respectively. For the experiment, 0.1 M of Na2SO4 was used as the electrolyte. For the photocurrent test, a 150 W Xe arc lamp equipped with an AM 1.5 G filter was used as the light source, illuminating the sample surface at a light intensity of 100 mW cm−2. The flat-band potential was measured at a scan frequency of 1 kHz. The color characteristics of the sample were characterized using a colorimeter (LS173, Linshang). The temperature variation of the sample when exposed to light was characterized using a thermal imaging camera (E50, FLIR). Solar radiation intensity data were acquired using a photoelectric total solar radiation sensor (SN-300AL-RA-N01). Total organic carbon (TOC) was measured using a TOC analyzer (vario TOC cube, Elementar) to evaluate the mineralization level of the dyes.

Stock solutions of MB and Rh B with an initial concentration of 1000 mg/L were prepared for the adsorption experiments. The desired dye concentrations were then achieved by diluting the stock solutions. All adsorption experiments were conducted at 20 °C. Each adsorption experiment was conducted in triplicate to ensure accuracy and reliability. Standard deviation was calculated for each set of replicates to demonstrate variability, and with the values depicted as error bars in the corresponding figures.

To access the adsorption kinetics of the samples, 20 mg of each sample was added to 100 mL of dye solution at an initial concentration of 60 mg/L, and the dye concentration was monitored at specific time intervals. For the adsorption isotherm studies, 6 mg of each sample was added to 20 mL of dye solution at concentrations ranging from 15 to 180 mg/L. The dye concentration was checked after 2.5 h. To investigate the reusability of the samples, 10 mg of adsorbent was added to 20 mL of 60 mg/L dye solution. After 2.5 h, the dye concentration was measured. The adsorbent was subsequently retrieved via centrifugation and then immersed in ethanol for another 2.5 h to facilitate dye desorption. The desorbed adsorbent was recovered via centrifugation, dried in an oven at 40 °C for 12 h, and employed in subsequent reuse experiments. Adsorption–desorption experiments were carried out over 5 cycles. The adsorption efficiency (%) was used as an indicator and calculated relative to the initial dye adsorption capacity of the sample as the standard. To evaluate the adsorption capacity of the samples at varying pH levels, 10 mg of each sample was added to 20 mL of 60 mg/L dye solution for 2.5 h. The pH of the dye solution was adjusted from 3 to 10 using 0.1 mol/L of NaOH and 0.1 mol/L of HCl. To investigate the effect of temperature on the adsorption capacity of the sample, an oil bath was used to control the temperature, and the remaining experimental conditions were the same as those for the pH experiment.

The adsorption capacity (q, mg/g) of the adsorbent was confirmed using the following equation:

MB and Rh B solutions at a concentration of 30 mg/L were first prepared using stock solutions from the adsorption experiments. Each photocatalytic degradation experiment was conducted in triplicate to ensure accuracy and reliability. Standard deviation was calculated for each set of replicates to demonstrate variability, and these values are represented as error bars in the corresponding figures.

In each experiment, 6 mg of the sample was added to 40 mL of dye solution with a concentration of 30 mg/L. The mixture was placed in the dark for 1 h to achieve adsorption–desorption equilibrium. Subsequently, the mixture was exposed to light for 1 h to observe the photocatalytic reaction. The dye concentration was monitored at specific time intervals. In this study, two light sources, UV light (KGW–94 N, KAIS) and natural sunlight, were utilized. For UV radiation, a light intensity of 30 mW cm−2 and a wavelength of 356 nm were used. The samples were placed 5 cm below the light source. For natural sunlight radiation, the samples were subjected to outdoor conditions with a light intensity of approximately 1.2 sun (Fig. S1). To assess the reusability of the samples, 20 mg of the sample was added to 40 mL of 30 mg/L dye solution. Other processes followed the same procedure. During the catalytic degradation experiments, a light intensity of 1.2 sun was generated through a solar simulator using a xenon lamp. Following the photocatalytic degradation experiment, the sample was collected via centrifugation to evaluate its reusability. Afterward, the sample was rinsed three times with DI water and dried in an oven at 40 °C for 12 h. The prepared sample was used in further reuse tests. The degradation efficiency (%) was used as an indicator, calculated using the initial dye degradation capacity of the sample as the standard.

Fig. 2 a shows the TEM image of the CDs derived from apple peel as the carbon source using a hydrothermal synthesis method. The image reveals well-defined particle structures with clear boundaries and a size distribution ranging from 2 to 6 nm. Fig. 2b shows the HR-TEM image of the CDs, confirming the crystalline nature of the CDs via a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (100) diffraction plane of graphite. This finding indicates that the synthesized CDs possess a graphite-like structure [35]. Given that the CDs are intended for photocatalytic applications in this study, their optical properties were further investigated. As shown in Fig. S2, the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of the CDs exhibited a prominent absorbance peak at 282 nm, attributable to the π–π* transitions of the aromatic C=C bond from the carbon core of the CDs [36]. The fluorescent properties of the prepared CDs are shown in Fig. S3. As identified in Fig. S3a, the fluorescence emission intensity reached a maximum of 430 nm under an excitation wavelength of 360 nm. The analysis of the excitation-dependent fluorescence emission spectra (Fig. S3b) revealed a strong correlation between the emission intensity of the CDs and the excitation wavelengths. Specifically, the emission peak intensity increased from 330 to 360 nm and gradually decreased from 360 to 480 nm. This behavior aligns with the findings of previous reports and is attributable to the size distribution of the CDs and the presence of various energy levels related to functional groups on the CD surface [36,37]. The photostability of the prepared CDs was assessed by evaluating their fluorescence properties after different storage periods and UV exposure durations; the results are presented in Figs. S3c and S3d. Notably, no significant changes in emission intensity were observed after 10 days of storage or 120 min of UV irradiation, demonstrating the excellent photostability of the prepared CDs. Overall, CDs with robust optical properties were successfully synthesized.

Fig. 2c shows the SEM image of CDs@BN. The morphology of BN was maintained after coating with CDs, similar to that of pure BN (Fig. S4). This result suggests that CD coating has minimal impact on the geometrical characteristics of BN, which is advantageous for the application of BN in water treatment. The elemental mapping image in Fig. 2d confirms the uniform distribution of CDs@BN within the fibers, suggesting the successful incorporation of CDs@BN into the electrospun PAN fibers. Such uniform distribution without significant aggregation of functional materials is beneficial for enhancing dye removal efficiency [38]. Fig. 2e shows the SEM image of the PAN/CDs@BN15 sample. The fibers exhibited a morphology with periodic protrusions. A closer examination shows that these protrusions primarily consist of loaded CDs@BN as well as pore structures (insert in Fig. 2e). Pure PAN fibers (Fig. S5a) have a smooth surface; however, after CDs@BN loading, protruding structures were observed (Fig. S5b). Increased CDs@BN concentrations resulted in more pronounced protrusions, as shown in Fig. S5c and Fig. 2e. This phenomenon occurs because the initial CDs@BN fills the fiber interior, and as the CDs@BN content rises, the excess material emerges at the fiber surface, forming protrusions and pore structures. Increased CDs@BN content generates more protrusions. Notably, these porous protrusions increase the specific surface area of the composite and facilitate effective exposure of functional materials, potentially enhancing dye removal efficiency. Although increased loading of functional materials can be advantageous, we found that increasing the CDs@BN content beyond 15 wt% led to nozzle clogging during electrospinning, hindering product formation. Therefore, a CDs@BN loading of 15 wt% was considered as the maximum loading in this study.

In addition to the generation of protruding structures, a change in fiber diameter was observed with increased CDs@BN loading, as demonstrated by the SEM images and quantitative analysis (Fig. 2f). When PAN fibers were loaded with 5 wt% CDs@BN (PAN/CDs@BN5), a slight increase in diameter was observed; this was not the case in the pure PAN fibers. This diameter increase is attributable to the CDs@BN loading within the fibers. However, as the CDs@BN content increased to 10 and 15 wt%, the fiber diameter did not increase significantly. This stabilization in diameter can be explained by the high electrical conductivity of CDs, which enhances the stretching of fibers under electrostatic forces during electrospinning and ultimately counteracts further diameter expansion [34]. Thus, the final diameter of CDs@BN-loaded fibers reflects a balance between the volumetric impact of the CDs@BN filling and the stretching effect induced by electrostatic forces. This effect was corroborated by assessments conducted on PAN/BN15 fibers (Fig. S5d and Fig. 2f), where the absence of CDs led to a marked increase in fiber diameter as the functional material content increased. In summary, the inclusion of CDs@BN not only generates a desirable porous structure on the fiber surface but also maintains the fiber diameter within an optimal range, which is essential for enhancing dye removal efficiency.

Fig. 2g shows the FTIR spectra of the representative samples. For pristine BN, three characteristic peaks were observed at 3395, 1335, and 755 cm−1, corresponding to the in-plane stretching vibration of O–H, B–N and out-of-plane stretching of B–N–B, respectively [39]. After coating with CDs, two additional characteristic peaks at 2966 and 1708 cm−1 were observed, corresponding to stretching vibrations of C–H and C=O, respectively [40]. The FTIR spectra of the CDs@BN-incorporated electrospun PAN fibers also revealed distinct characteristic peaks at 1445 and 784 cm−1, similar to those of CDs@BN; however, these peaks were absent in the pure PAN fibers. This confirms the successful incorporation of CDs@BN into the electrospun fibers. The characteristic diffraction peaks at 26.6°, 41.7°, 54.9°, and 76° in the XRD spectra correspond to the hexagonal crystal planes of BN [41]. Both BN and CDs@BN display these peaks, albeit with slight intensity variations, indicating that CD-assisted exfoliation of BN results in modifications in layer spacing [39]. Furthermore, the PAN/CDs@BN15 samples exhibited prominent diffraction peaks at 26.6°, which were not observed for pure PAN fibers. This result further confirmed the successful incorporation of CDs@BN into the electrospun PAN matrix.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of the dye adsorption performance
ce:section.ce:para: The initial analysis of the obtained samples indicated highly promising physicochemical properties for dye removal. Consequently, the adsorption and photocatalytic degradation performances of the prepared samples were systematically investigated. In this study, MB and Rh B dyes were chosen as model pollutants because of their widespread presence in daily life and industrial effluents [1]. Among the various parameters used to assess adsorption performance, adsorption rate and capacity were identified as the most critical factors. Therefore, they were evaluated first, and the results are shown in Fig. 3 . Fig. 3a depicts the relationship between the contact time of the adsorbent with the MB solution and its corresponding adsorption capacity. The data indicate that, for all the samples, the adsorption process was rapid at the initial stage and then slowly approached to equilibrium as the experiment progressed. This phenomenon is attributable to the abundance of active functional groups on the adsorbent surface, which facilitates immediate interaction with dye molecules, resulting in swift initial adsorption. As the active sites on the adsorbents become increasingly occupied, the adsorption capacity eventually reaches saturation. Notably, equilibrium was achieved within approximately 1 h for all samples, demonstrating the excellent adsorption rate of the synthesized adsorbents. The adsorption behavior of the samples toward Rh B dye (Fig. 3b) was similar to that toward MB. This consistency indicates the robust and versatile adsorption performance of the fabricated samples. Two adsorption kinetic models, pseudo-first-order and pseudo-second-order models, were used to further evaluate the adsorption behavior of the adsorbent [2]. Their fittings are demonstrated in Figs. 3a, 3b, and S6, and the fitting parameters are summarized in Tables S1 and S2. After assessing the goodness-of-fit (R2 values) and the consistency between the experimental data and the models, it was determined that the pseudo-second-order model provided a superior description of the adsorption behavior for both dyes. This finding suggests that chemisorption involving hydrogen bonding, electrostatic interaction, and π–π interactions predominates the adsorption process [42,43].

The effect of the initial MB concentration on the adsorption capacity of the adsorbent is demonstrated in Fig. 3c. The adsorption capacity of the samples gradually increased as the dye concentration increased and eventually saturated. This behavior is attributable to the finite number of functional sites on the adsorbent surface; once these sites were fully occupied by dye molecules, further increases in dye concentration exerted minimal influence on the adsorption capacity. Additionally, the incorporation of CDs@BN significantly enhanced the adsorption capacity of the composite adsorbent. Specifically, as the CDs@BN content increased, a marked improvement in adsorption capacity was observed, with PAN/CDs@BN15 exhibiting the highest adsorption capacity in this study. This enhancement is attributed to the contribution of CDs@BN to the overall adsorption process, where a higher content of CDs@BN corresponds to an increased number of active adsorption sites. In contrast, the adsorption capacity of the PAN/BN15 sample was substantially lower than that of the PAN/CDs@BN15 sample, highlighting the effectiveness of CD coating on BN in improving adsorption performance. A similar adsorption behavior was also confirmed upon Rh B adsorption (Fig. 3d), further demonstrating the excellent performance of the sample. To determine the maximum adsorption capacity of the samples for the dyes, two commonly used adsorption isotherm models, Langmuir and Freundlich models, were used [2]. Their fittings are presented in Figs. 3c, 3d, and S7, and the fitting parameters are summarized in Tables S3 and S4. The goodness-of-fit (R2 values) data and the alignment of the experimental data with the models indicated that the adsorption behavior of the samples for both dyes aligns more closely with the Langmuir model. This indicates that homogeneous adsorption sites were present on the adsorbent and uniform interactions were formed between the adsorbed species [44]. According to the Langmuir model, the PAN/CDs@BN15 sample exhibited the highest adsorption capacities for both dyes, with 220.79 mg/g for MB and 198.83 mg/g for Rh B. These values were approximately double those of the PAN/BN15 adsorbent, further underscoring the versatility and efficacy of CD coating in enhancing adsorption performance.

Based on the evaluation of sample performance, PAN/CDs@BN15 demonstrated superior adsorption characteristics compared to the other samples. As a result, this sample was used to further evaluate the variation of its adsorption capacity under different environmental conditions. In real-world dye-containing wastewater effluents, the pH value is often non-neutral because of the presence of various contaminants. The pH of the wastewater can significantly influence the interaction between the functional groups on the adsorbent and the dye molecules, thereby affecting the final adsorption capacity [45]. To explore this effect, the adsorption performance of the sample was evaluated across a pH range of 3–10, and the results are presented in Fig. 4 a. The pH level of the solution significantly affected the adsorption capacity of the samples. For MB, the adsorption capacity of the samples gradually increased as the pH value increased from 3 to 10. This trend is attributable to the competition between the cationic dye molecules and excess H+ ions in the solution. At elevated pH levels, the reduced competition from H+ ions facilitates interactions between the adsorbent and dye molecules, resulting in improved adsorption performance [46]. In contrast, for Rh B, the adsorption capacity of the sample increased as the pH level increased from 3 to 8 but decreased beyond this point. This behavior can be explained by the molecular structure of Rh B, which includes carboxylic groups that become negatively charged under alkaline conditions. At elevated pH levels, electrostatic repulsion occurred between the negatively charged Rh B molecules and the surface of the adsorbent, leading to a decline in adsorption capacity [47]. Notably, for both dyes, the adsorption capacity of the sample approached maximum under neutral pH conditions, representative of most real-world wastewater scenarios. This observation underscores the practicability and robustness of the fabricated adsorbents for treating dye-contaminated wastewater under diverse environmental conditions.

In real-world scenarios, dye-containing wastewater streams may vary in temperature, alongside different pH environments. Moreover, this study incorporates the photocatalytic degradation of dyes under solar irradiation, which can lead to an increase in the dye solution temperature. As a result, the effect of temperature on the adsorption capacity of the samples was investigated. As shown in Fig. 4b, the adsorption capacity of the adsorbent for both dyes exhibited a slight enhancement with increased temperatures, suggesting that the adsorption process is endothermic. This is attributable to the increase in the diffusion rate of dye molecules due to elevated temperatures. The enhanced molecular mobility provides the dye molecules with sufficient energy to interact more effectively with the functional groups on the adsorbent surface and facilitates access to the internal pore structure, resulting in an increased adsorption capacity [48]. These results further validate the exceptional practicability of the fabricated adsorbents.

Reusability is a critical parameter in evaluating the performance of adsorbents, as it reduces operational costs and minimizes waste generation. Fig. 4c shows the change in the adsorption capacity of the samples over multiple reuse cycles. After five cycles, the removal efficiency of the adsorbent for both dyes remained consistently high, retaining approximately 95 % of its original capacity. The SEM image of the sample after the cycling experiment is displayed in Fig. S8. No significant change in morphology was noticed for the samples; this is beneficial for stabilizing performance. This finding highlights the excellent reusability of the prepared samples, underscoring their potential for real-world applications. Fig. 4d presents a comparative analysis of the adsorption capacities of the fabricated adsorbents for MB and Rh B dyes relative to those reported in recently published studies [45,46,49–56]. For both dyes, the results of the current work outperformed or are comparable to those of most reports (see details in Table S5). This superior performance underscores the high competitiveness of the fabricated samples and their viability for practical dye removal applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Exploration of the dye adsorption mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: To elucidate the remarkable dye adsorption performance of the samples, a series of characterizations were conducted to investigate the underlying adsorption mechanisms. Fig. 5 a depicts the FTIR spectra of the samples before and after dye adsorption. In contrast to the pristine samples, significant alterations were observed in the characteristic peaks of the dye-laden samples, indicating effective interactions between the surface functional groups of the adsorbent and the dye molecules. For example, upon dye adsorption, the O–H stretching vibrations shifted from 3630 to 3637 and 3635 cm−1 for MB and Rh B, respectively. These changes were attributed to hydrogen bonding, with the abundant nitrogen-containing groups within the test dyes acting as hydrogen bond acceptors [57]. Additionally, the fluctuation of the peaks within the 1800–1600 cm−1 range before and after adsorption was attributed to the interactions of C=O groups and aromatic rings of the dye molecules [34]. Furthermore, the peak fluctuations within the 1100–1300 cm−1 range are associated with symmetric deformation of –CH3 groups and C–N bonds [58]. The high-resolution C1s and O1s XPS spectra are shown in Fig. 5b and 5c, respectively. As shown in Fig. 5b, the peaks at binding energies of 283.00, 284.60, and 285.80 eV correspond to C–C, C–H, and C=C; C–N, C=N, and C–O–C; and O–C–O, C–O, and C=O functional groups, respectively [59]. Upon dye adsorption, noticeable shifts in the binding energies were observed, indicating the involvement of these functional groups in the adsorption process. The O1s spectrum of the sample can be resolved into three peaks, as shown in Fig. 5c. The peaks at 529.65, 532.00, and 533.80 eV correspond to the C–O, –OH and C=O species, respectively [34]. These binding energies also exhibited notable shifts upon dye adsorption, further confirming the participation of these groups in the adsorption process. The XPS findings are consistent with the FTIR analysis results, collectively validating that the abundant functional groups on the adsorbent surface interact effectively with the dye molecules, resulting in excellent adsorption capacity.

The specific surface area and pore characteristics of an adsorbent are critical parameters that influence its performance, as they directly affect both the quantity and effectiveness of functional groups available on the adsorbent surface [2]. Therefore, nitrogen adsorption and desorption isotherms (Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET)) were analyzed to further characterize these properties (Fig. 5d–f). The results revealed that all samples exhibited predominantly mesoporous structures. The PAN/CDs@BN15 sample exhibited the largest pore volume and specific surface area compared to the other samples. This enhancement is attributable to the inherently high specific surface area and pore volume of BN, which was further amplified by CD coating (Fig. S9a). Furthermore, the pore size distribution showed that PAN/CDs@BN15 exhibited a higher proportion of mesopores (Fig. S9b), especially in the 2–10 nm range, than PAN and PAN/BN15, which allowed for better reactant diffusion and active site exposure. These findings highlight that the incorporation of CDs not only increases the pore volume and specific surface area but also optimizes the pore structure of the adsorbent, thereby enhancing the loading of functional groups on the surface as well as the efficient exposure of these groups. This consequently improves the adsorption performance of the material.

The hydrophilicity of the adsorbent also plays a pivotal role in determining its adsorption capacity, as dyes in aqueous solutions are more likely to penetrate hydrophilic adsorbents and interact more efficiently with their functional groups, thus enhancing adsorption performance [1]. Fig. 5g presents optical images illustrating the wetting behavior of DI water, MB solution, and Rh B solution on the sample surface. The results indicate that all tested liquids wet the samples, confirming the hydrophilic nature of the fabricated adsorbent. This hydrophilicity is attributable to the inherent properties of the PAN substrate and the presence of CDs. These findings underscore the rationality of the material selection for the fabricated adsorbent and its role in further enhancing adsorption capacity.

Overall, a schematic illustrating the adsorption mechanism of the fabricated adsorbent with excellent dye adsorption performance is presented in Fig. 5h. The CDs@BN functional materials are characterized by an abundance of surface functional groups, which remain effectively exposed even after being integrated into electrospun PAN fibers. These functional groups fully interact with dye molecules through various mechanisms, including hydrogen bonding, π–π interactions, and electrostatic interactions, thereby significantly enhancing dye adsorption capabilities.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of the photocatalytic degradation performance of the dyes
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 6 a shows the removal performance of the sample for MB under various test conditions. Notably, the dye removal effect comprises two mechanisms: adsorption and photocatalytic degradation. Therefore, the overall dye removal performance represents the combined contribution of adsorption-derived and photocatalytic degradation-derived removal. Overall, all samples containing CDs@BN demonstrated improved dye removal performance with increased CDs@BN loading under all test conditions. This finding underscores the combined effectiveness of adsorption and photocatalysis in dye removal as well as the efficacy of CDs@BN in enhancing performance. When subjected to UV illumination, the PAN/CDs@BN15 sample exhibited a photocatalytic degradation efficiency for MB that was approximately double that of the PAN/BN15 sample. The relatively low degradation efficiency of PAN/BN15 under UV light was attributed to the inherent limitations of BN [16]. In contrast, the significantly improved performance of PAN/CDs@BN15 highlights the effectiveness of CD coating in promoting MB photocatalytic degradation. Under natural sunlight, the photocatalytic degradation performance of all samples containing CDs@BN was further enhanced; the degradation performance surpassed that observed under UV illumination and exceeded the adsorption-derived removal contribution. For the PAN/BN15 sample, exposure to natural sunlight increased the photocatalytic degradation performance only slightly compared to UV light exposure; this is attributable to the BN-induced photosensitization effect of the dye under visible light [60]. However, the degradation efficiency of the PAN/BN15 sample under natural sunlight was only about one-fifth of that achieved by the PAN/CDs@BN15 sample. The PAN/CDs@BN15 sample demonstrated an impressive 99.50 % MB removal efficiency under natural sunlight due to the combined effects of photocatalytic degradation and adsorption. These results confirm the significant enhancement in the dye photocatalytic degradation performance of the BN through CD coating.

Fig. 6b shows the kinetic curves of the PAN/CDs@BN15 sample during the photocatalytic degradation of MB. The MB concentration remained relatively unchanged under light irradiation alone, implying that the observed reduction in MB concentration is primarily attributed to its catalytic degradation facilitated by the catalyst. Additionally, sunlight exposure had a more pronounced catalytic effect on MB degradation than UV light exposure. Under UV light, the concentration of MB gradually decreased throughout the experiment, whereas sunlight catalysis achieved equilibrium within approximately 40 min. Fig. 6c shows the temporal variation in the absorbance of MB solution during the photocatalytic process using the PAN/CDs@BN15 sample under sunlight. After 60 min of reaction, the absorbance of the MB solution approached zero, and the solution became nearly transparent. This observation strongly corroborates the efficient MB removal efficiency of the catalyst.

Fig. 6d shows the removal efficiency of the sample for Rh B under different test environments. The results revealed a removal trend similar to that observed for MB. In particular, during sunlight-assisted catalytic degradation, the PAN/CDs@BN15 sample exhibited the maximum removal efficiency for Rh B, achieving a removal rate of 85.9 % due to its combined catalytic and adsorption performance. This slightly reduced efficiency (compared to MB degradation) is attributable to the differences in the interactions between the functional groups on the sample surface and the molecular structures of the two dyes [61]. The kinetic curves for the photocatalytic degradation of Rh B by the PAN/CDs@BN15 sample are displayed in Fig. 6e. Similar to the MB results, sunlight exposure catalyzed the degradation of Rh B more effectively than UV light exposure. Additionally, as the experiment proceeded, the dye concentration gradually decreased under both light sources. The UV–vis absorbance curves and visual color changes of the Rh B solution are displayed in Fig. 6f. The concentration of Rh B gradually decreased, and the color shifted from dark pink to light pink as the experiment proceeded. These findings collectively highlight the efficacy of CD coating in developing a highly competitive and rational approach for dye photocatalytic degradation.

In order to assess the mineralization rate of the organic matter in solution, we monitored the variations in TOC before and after the photocatalytic degradation of the dye. The results showed that the photocatalytic treatment induced significant mineralization of the dyes. Specifically, for MB, the TOC concentration decreased from 32.05 mg/L to 12.33 mg/L, indicating a mineralization rate of 61.53 %. For Rh B, the TOC reduction was from 35.33 mg/L to 11.08 mg/L, corresponding to a mineralization rate of 68.64 %. These data suggest that a substantial fraction of the dyes was effectively mineralized and decomposed into benign byproducts, such as water and carbon dioxide, through photocatalytic degradation, thus highlighting the high efficiency of the synthesized photocatalyst.

To quantitatively assess the impact of different light sources on the photocatalytic degradation performance of the PAN/CDs@BN15 sample, the degradation data were fitted to a pseudo-first-order kinetic model [62], as illustrated in Fig. S10. The fitting results indicate that the degradation kinetics align well with the pseudo-first-order model. By fitting the experimental data, the catalytic degradation rates k (min−1) of the sample under different light conditions were determined; the findings are summarized in Fig. 6g. For both dyes, the degradation rate under sunlight-assisted catalysis was markedly higher than that under UV light. Furthermore, the catalytic degradation rate for MB consistently surpassed that for Rh B under both light condition; this was consistent with the experimental observations discussed earlier.

Reusability is a critical parameter for evaluating the practicability of catalysts. This aspect of the sample was further investigated, and the results are presented in Fig. 6h. Notably, the catalytic degradation efficiency for both dyes retained over 90 % of the initial performance after five cycles of use. This finding underscores the excellent reusability of the prepared catalyst, highlighting its viability for long-term practical applications. Fig. 6i presents a comparative analysis of the dye removal performance of the fabricated catalyst for MB and Rh B, relative to catalysts immobilized in electrospun substrates reported in recent literature [61–66]. The results demonstrate that the catalyst developed in this study exhibits degradation rates and removal efficiencies that are highly competitive with or superior to those reported previously (see details in Table S6). These outcomes emphasize the exceptional potential of the prepared catalyst for practical dye degradation applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Exploration of the dye photocatalytic degradation mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: The dye photocatalytic degradation experiments described above reveal that the incorporation of CDs significantly enhanced the performance of BN. A series of characterizations were conducted to elucidate the mechanisms responsible for the superior catalytic performance of the synthesized materials. As depicted in Fig. 7 a, both BN and CDs@BN exhibited prominent absorption bands in the 200–300 nm range; these bands are attributable to the excitation of σ electron within the B–N bond [67]. Additionally, CDs@BN exhibited stronger absorptions than pure BN across the UV–vis range. The inset represents the band gap energies of the two samples evaluated using Tauc plots [68]. The CDs@BN sample exhibited a lower band gap energy (4.41 eV) than pure BN (5.07 eV). This suggests that CD-coated BN can efficiently absorb UV and visible light from sunlight irradiation, ultimately enhancing its catalytic performance [68]. To further investigate the impact of CDs on the photoelectric properties of BN, the transient photocurrent response was analyzed, and the results are presented in Fig. 7b. Increased photocurrent levels are typically indicative of improved separation efficiency of photogenerated electron–hole (e−–h+) pairs within a photocatalyst, leading to enhanced photocatalytic performance. Among the samples, the pristine BN sample exhibited the lowest photocurrent density (near-zero), attributable to the rapid recombination of photogenerated e−–h+ pairs [65]. In contrast, the incorporation of CDs resulted in a significant increase in photocurrent response. This enhancement indicates that CDs@BN facilitates efficient carrier migration and effectively suppresses charge recombination [65]. Furthermore, based on the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) results, CDs@BN resulted in lower interfacial resistance and faster carrier migration compared to pristine BN, further indicating a higher charge separation efficiency (Fig. 7c). Both materials were subjected to Mott-Schottky analysis and showed positive slopes in the potential curves, confirming their n-type semiconductor characteristics (Fig. S11). The flat band potentials (E FB) of CDs@BN and BN were determined to be −0.59 eV and −0.52 eV (vs. Ag/AgCl), respectively. Upon conversion to the normal hydrogen electrode (NHE) scale using the relationship E(NHE) = E(Ag/AgCl) + 0.197 V, the corresponding E FB values shifted close to −0.39 eV and −0.32 eV (vs. NHE). For n-type semiconductors, the E FB is typically 0.2 eV above the conduction band edge (E CB). Accordingly, the conduction band minima of CDs@BN and BN were estimated to be −0.59 eV and −0.52 eV (vs. NHE), respectively. Based on these values, the valence band positions (E VB) were calculated to be 3.82 eV and 4.55 eV (vs. NHE), respectively [68]. The Mott-Schottky analysis revealed significant band structure changes in CDs@BN compared to BN, indicating the formation of a heterojunction. The negative shift in the conduction band position suggests improved electron transfer capability, while the lower valence band position enhances oxidation potential. Such a staggered band arrangement indicates a potential Type-II heterojunction [69]. Under light irradiation, electrons are excited to the conduction bands of both BN and CDs. The band alignment facilitates electron migration from BN to CDs and hole migration from CDs to BN, effectively suppressing charge recombination. The accumulated electrons in CDs react with O2 to form superoxide radicals, while holes in BN promote oxidation reactions. Efficient charge separation and utilization significantly enhance photocatalytic dye degradation under light irradiation.

In the photocatalytic degradation reaction, the catalytic performance of the catalyst was significantly affected by active species, including hydroxyl radicals (·OH), holes (h+), and superoxide radicals (·O2 −) [64]. To further understand the photocatalytic degradation mechanism of the samples for dye removal, scavenger tests were conducted. For the scavenger experiments, the material dosage and experimental conditions were identical to those used in the reuse tests, with the exception that specific scavengers were introduced into the dye solution before the reaction. IPA, EDTA, and BQ (each at a concentration of 1 mM) were used as trapping agents for ·OH, h+, and ·O2 −, respectively [61,62]. As shown in Fig. 7d, the addition of scavengers significantly affected the degradation efficiency of both dyes. For MB, the addition of EDTA showed the greatest degradation inhibition effect, followed by IPA and BQ. Similarly, for RhB, EDTA exhibited the strongest inhibitory effect on degradation, followed by BQ and IPA. These findings indicate that h+ functions as the primary active species in the photocatalytic degradation of both dyes by the synthesized samples.

When incorporating functional materials with distinct colors into the electrospun membrane, the membrane may exhibit color variations depending on the amount of functional materials added. This color change can influence the light absorption ability of the membrane and consequently affect the photocatalytic degradation performance. However, this aspect has rarely been addressed in previous studies. In this study, the prepared CDs@BN functional materials had a light-black color (Fig. 1a), suggesting that varying concentrations of CDs@BN could alter the color of the membranes. Here, we investigated two factors that could influence the photocatalytic degradation performance to induce membrane color variations: the light reflection behavior and the associated thermal effects. The color variation across the samples was evaluated using total color differences (ΔE); pure electrospun PAN was used as the reference [70]. A high ΔE value is indicative of a dark membrane color. As shown in Fig. 7e, the PAN/BN15 sample exhibited no notable color change compared to the reference sample; this is attributable to the white color of the BN powder (Fig. 1a). When CDs@BN was incorporated into the electrospun membrane, the color of the membrane changed significantly and became darker as the CDs@BN content was increased. This observation is consistent with the anticipated effects, as CDs@BN inherently exhibits a blackish coloration. Fig. 7f shows the UV–vis reflectance spectra of the samples. For the PAN/BN15 samples, the reflectance exceeded that of pure PAN, possibly due to the high scattering efficiency of BN [71]. This increased reflectance reduces UV and visible light absorption, potentially leading to diminished photocatalytic degradation performance. Conversely, the incorporation of CDs@BN led to a substantial decrease in light reflectance, reaching a minimum in the PAN/CDs@BN15 sample. The reduction in reflectance enhanced the adsorption of UV and visible light by the optimal sample, facilitating the generation of photogenerated electron–hole pairs and improving the photocatalytic degradation efficiency. The thermal effects of the membrane color were evaluated, and the results are presented in Fig. 7g. Under light exposure, the temperature of the PAN/BN15 sample was lower than that of pure PAN, possibly due to the high scattering efficiency of BN; this is consistent with the results in Fig. 7f. However, the PAN/CDs@BN15 sample demonstrated a significantly higher temperature than the PAN/BN15 sample under identical light exposure conditions. This increase in temperature is attributable to the relatively low light reflectivity of the PAN/CDs@BN15 sample (Fig. 7f). The enhanced thermal effect of the optimized sample facilitates fast reaction kinetics, contributing to an improved catalytic degradation rate. Overall, the CDs@BN-induced alteration in the color of the electrospun membrane enhanced the photocatalytic degradation of the dye. This observation is consistent with the experimental results, further supporting the hypothesis that membrane coloration, through its influence on light absorption and thermal effects, plays a critical role in optimizing catalytic efficiency.

Fig. 7h presents a schematic illustration of the photocatalytic degradation mechanism for the samples prepared in this study. The incorporation of CDs has been shown to enhance charge separation, facilitate electron transfer, and promote the generation of reactive oxygen species. CDs reduce the band gap of BN, improving light absorption and extending its response into the visible region. The formation of a heterojunction effectively suppresses charge recombination. This process enhances the generation of ·O2 − and ·OH, with photogenerated holes (h+) playing a dominant role in the degradation reaction. In addition, CDs reduce light reflectance and enhance heat accumulation, which further accelerates the reaction kinetics. Furthermore, CDs@BN exhibited optimal specific surface area and desired wettability, as evidenced by the results of the adsorption mechanism investigation. These synergistic factors collectively contributed to the superior photocatalytic degradation performance of the synthesized samples.

In summary, we successfully fabricated CDs@BN composites and incorporated them into electrospun PAN fibers to achieve efficient dye adsorption and sunlight-driven photocatalytic degradation. The synergistic interaction between the CDs and BN significantly enhanced the dye adsorption capabilities. The optimized samples demonstrated remarkable adsorption capacities of 220.79 mg/g for MB and 198.81 mg/g for Rh B, which are nearly double those of uncoated BN. These values are also superior to many recently published work [45,46,49–56]. Furthermore, the optimized adsorbents exhibited outstanding performance across various adsorption environments, outperforming numerous recently reported systems. CDs@BN showed significantly improved photocatalytic activity for light-assisted photocatalytic degradation. Specifically, the optimized samples achieved removal efficiencies of 99.50 % for MB and 85.90 % for Rh B within 1 h of solar irradiation. Additionally, competitive catalytic degradation rates of 0.07 min−1 for MB and 0.03 min−1 for Rh B were recorded. These performances are also outperforming many recent contributions [61–66]. The mechanisms underlying the excellent performance of the prepared samples for dye adsorption and photocatalytic degradation were thoroughly explored. All the results indicate that coating BN with CDs serves as a highly efficient and competitive approach for dye removal. This innovative strategy paves the way for the development of advanced BN-based functional materials tailored for wastewater treatment applications. For practical large-scale applications, future work should prioritize the scalable synthesis of CDs@BN, process integration, and assessment of long-term environmental impacts to enable successful industrial application of CDs@BN-based wastewater treatment materials.

Shichen Li: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – original draft, Visualization. Bong-Kee Lee: Conceptualization, Resources, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Validation, Project administration, Funding acquisition.
